skill.name: cloud-penetration-testing
skill.description: Guides authorized cloud security assessments across Azure, AWS, GCP, and Microsoft 365 using reconnaissance, enumeration, exploitation, and reporting workflows.
skill.summary: Run structured, authorized cloud penetration tests across major cloud platforms.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings are largely true positives because the skill provides operational cloud penetration testing commands, including credential extraction, metadata-token retrieval, password spraying, persistence, and privilege escalation. Some keyword findings, such as weak cryptography and ransomware-extension matches, appear to be scanner noise from cloud security terminology and output filenames. No prompt injection attempt was found, but the confirmed offensive workflows make this high risk and not safe to publish without strict marketplace controls.

high_findings.title: Credential and Token Extraction Workflows
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to import stolen Azure context files, dump Key Vault secrets, extract Lambda environment variables, access cloud metadata services, and copy local GCP credential directories. These are high-risk credential access techniques that can expose secrets and enable account takeover outside an authorized engagement.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections explicitly describe stolen context import, secret dumping, metadata credential access, and copying credential stores. The legal-scope language reduces malicious intent certainty, but the techniques are clearly dual-use and dangerous.

high_findings.title: Persistence and Privilege Escalation Instructions
high_findings.description: The skill includes steps to create backdoor service principals, add identities to Global Admin, create new cloud users, reset service principal credentials, and assign directory roles through Microsoft Graph. These actions can establish unauthorized persistence and administrative control.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text labels the workflow as persistence and uses terms such as backdoor and Global Admin. This is strong semantic evidence of high-risk offensive capability.

high_findings.title: Password Spraying with IP Rotation
high_findings.description: The references provide a password spraying loop and the main skill demonstrates MSOLSpray with FireProx IP rotation. This can facilitate credential attacks against Azure or Microsoft 365 tenants.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands read user and password lists, attempt cloud logins, write valid credentials, and route activity through FireProx. The behavior directly matches credential attack automation.

high_findings.title: Cloud Data Access and Exfiltration Patterns
high_findings.description: The skill includes commands to download bucket contents, clone source repositories, retrieve function configuration, and export automation runbooks or job outputs. These workflows can extract sensitive data from cloud environments.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections extract configuration, repositories, bucket contents, environment variables, and user data. These can be legitimate assessment steps, but they create clear data exposure risk.

medium_findings.title: Installer Commands with Elevated Privileges
medium_findings.description: The setup instructions include downloading installers with curl, piping the GCP installer to bash, and running the AWS installer with sudo. These are common setup patterns but can execute remote code with elevated privileges.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are standard cloud CLI installation steps, so they are not inherently malicious. They still carry supply-chain and privileged execution risk if copied blindly.

medium_findings.title: Broad Reconnaissance and Enumeration Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill provides extensive cloud resource, identity, network, and metadata enumeration commands. This is expected for penetration testing, but it can support unauthorized discovery when used outside scope.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are legitimate for authorized audits and do not by themselves exploit targets. The breadth across identity, storage, compute, and network resources raises moderate misuse risk.

low_findings.title: Keyword-Only Static Matches Dismissed
low_findings.description: Several static matches for weak cryptography, ransomware extensions, and command execution are caused by security terminology, cloud command examples, or generated output filenames. I did not find evidence of malware payloads, obfuscation, or destructive file encryption behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss encrypted or unencrypted cloud resources and defensive constraints. The scanner keywords are present, but semantic context does not show ransomware or weak-crypto implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Metadata Service Credential Retrieval
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directly references AWS, Azure, and GCP metadata service endpoints used to retrieve instance identity or credential material.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These are exact cloud metadata endpoints and credential-access commands. The pattern is high confidence because the surrounding headings describe exploitation and token retrieval.

dangerous_patterns.title: Backdoor Identity Creation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes commands for creating service principals, users, access keys, and Global Admin assignments for persistence.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The content labels these actions as persistence and includes explicit backdoor wording. This confirms the dangerous pattern beyond static matching.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Attack Automation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill and reference file show password spraying, valid credential logging, and IP rotation through FireProx.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is explicit and operational. It has authorized testing uses but can directly enable account compromise.

content.value_statement: Cloud penetration tests require structured evidence, platform-specific checks, and strict authorization. This skill organizes Azure, AWS, GCP, and Microsoft 365 assessment workflows for approved security teams.
actual_capabilities: Provides phased workflows for cloud reconnaissance, authentication review, enumeration, exploitation validation, and reporting.
Covers Azure, AWS, GCP, and Microsoft 365 assessment commands and decision points.
Lists tools such as ScoutSuite, Pacu, AzureHound, ROADTools, and cloud_enum for authorized audits.
Helps identify exposed secrets, metadata access paths, IAM issues, storage exposure, and public resources.
Includes report-oriented deliverables such as resource inventories, credential findings, and remediation recommendations.
limitations: It must only be used with explicit written authorization and a defined scope.
It includes offensive techniques that may trigger cloud security monitoring and account controls.
It does not replace legal review, rules of engagement, or customer approval.
It does not guarantee exploit success because permissions, MFA, regions, and provider controls vary.

use_cases.title: Plan an Authorized Cloud Assessment
use_cases.description: Build a scoped test plan that maps Azure, AWS, and GCP checks to approved objectives and reporting needs.

use_cases.title: Review Cloud IAM Exposure
use_cases.description: Enumerate identities, roles, subscriptions, projects, accounts, and permissions to find excessive access.

use_cases.title: Validate Remediation Work
use_cases.description: Retest previously reported misconfigurations and confirm that secrets, storage, and metadata paths are closed.

prompt_templates.title: Define Assessment Scope
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me turn this authorized cloud assessment scope into a phased test plan. Include objectives, allowed accounts, excluded systems, evidence to collect, and stop conditions.

prompt_templates.title: Create Enumeration Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Azure, AWS, and GCP enumeration checklist for my approved environment. Focus on IAM, storage, compute, networking, logging, and evidence capture.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Cloud Findings
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these cloud assessment notes and group findings by risk, affected service, exploitability, business impact, and remediation priority.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Executive Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert these authorized cloud penetration test results into an executive summary, technical findings, evidence list, remediation plan, and validation checklist.

output_examples.input: Plan an authorized AWS and Azure assessment for one production account and one test tenant.
output_examples.output: A phased assessment plan with scope boundaries, prerequisites, IAM review tasks, storage checks, metadata exposure checks, logging considerations, evidence needs, and stop conditions.

output_examples.input: Summarize findings from cloud enumeration notes.
output_examples.output: A risk-ranked findings summary that separates excessive IAM privileges, exposed storage, secrets in configuration, weak monitoring, and recommended remediation owners.

output_examples.input: Prepare a retest checklist for fixed cloud misconfigurations.
output_examples.output: A validation checklist covering identity cleanup, secret rotation, storage access controls, metadata protections, audit logging, and evidence required for closure.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization, cloud account boundaries, time windows, and escalation contacts before testing.
Use least-privilege test credentials and record every command, timestamp, account, and region touched.
Coordinate high-risk tests such as password spraying, metadata access, and persistence validation with defenders first.
anti_patterns: Do not run credential attacks, persistence steps, or data downloads outside the approved scope.
Do not copy production customer data when metadata, configuration, or screenshots are enough evidence.
Do not leave new users, keys, service principals, local files, or downloaded data after testing.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for general marketplace publication?
faq.answer: No. The audit found high-risk offensive workflows that require strict authorization and expert supervision.

faq.question: Which cloud platforms does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Azure, AWS, GCP, and Microsoft 365 assessment activities.

faq.question: Does it require cloud credentials?
faq.answer: Yes. Most checks require approved credentials, tokens, or role access for the target environment.

faq.question: Can it produce remediation guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports evidence collection and remediation recommendations for common cloud weaknesses.

faq.question: What controls should users apply?
faq.answer: Users should apply written scope, legal approval, least privilege, logging, cleanup, and defender coordination.

faq.question: Does it include exploit techniques?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes credential access, metadata access, password spraying, persistence, and privilege escalation workflows.
